advaita.rendering: The Lord (Īśvara) adorned with celestial garlands and raiment (divya-mālya-āmbara), fragrant with divine unguents (divya-gandha-anulepana), manifests here as the form that is all-wonder (sarva-āścarya-maya) — not a personal being among beings but the boundless (ananta) substratum whose face is everywhere (viśvato-mukha) because all faces are nothing other than its own expression. Śaṅkara underlines that ananta denies every limit: no terminus in space, time, or quality. This universal-facedness signals not a body with many heads but the non-dual ground (advaya-tattva) whose presence constitutes the very structure of manifestation.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara: 'na asya antaḥ asti iti anantaḥ'; 'viśvato-mukhaṃ sarva-bhūta-ātma-bhūtatvāt' — boundlessness established by self-identity with all beings, not by spatial extension.
advaita.divergence_note: Advaita reads the cosmic form as a pedagogical concession to Arjuna's dualistic vision; the form is not Brahman's true nature.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: The radiant Lord (deva — he who effulgently shines, dyotamāna) is ananta in the sense of transcending all three times and being the indwelling support (āśraya) of the entire universe; his faces turned in every direction (viśva-dik-varti-mukha) declare that every quarter of the cosmos is sustained within his body. The divine garlands, fragrance, and ornaments are not ornamental detail but his own sovereign attributes (sva-ucita-divya-āmbara-gandha-mālā-ābharaṇa-āyudha), marks of supreme lordship (aiśvarya) permanently belonging to his divine body. Rāmānuja's next sentence promises to specify the effulgence (dyotamānatā) further — the form is the very luminosity of Bhagavān's self-revelation.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Unlike Advaita, Viśiṣṭādvaita insists the divine ornaments are real attributes of a real personal body, not accommodations to lower understanding.

dvaita.rendering: This is the Lord Hari whose form is constituted entirely of wonder (sarva-āścarya-ātmaka) — every element of it, from the celestial garlands to the infinite faces, is genuinely astonishing because Hari's own nature exceeds every category of ordinary existence. The jīva perceives and worships this form from a position of absolute dependence (pāratiṃtrya); no aspect of the cosmic form belongs to the jīva or is the jīva. Madhva's terse gloss — 'sarva-āścarya-ātmakam' — is a precision move: the form is not merely wonderful-seeming but ontologically wonder-constituted, affirming the radical qualitative difference (viśeṣa) between Hari and every other category of being.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva: 'sarva-āścarya-mayaṃ sarva-āścarya-ātmakam' — the compound is read as the form's own essential constitution, not an observer-relative quality.
dvaita.divergence_note: Bhāṣya here is minimal (one gloss); remaining inferences follow standard Dvaita doctrine on Hari's absolute independence and jīva's eternal distinction.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Vallabha reads this form as the Mahākāla-puruṣa (cosmic time-person) displayed as the path of maryādā-mārga — the ordered, rule-bound approach to the divine — whose celestial garment (āmbara) is itself made of the Vedic metres (chandas-māya-rūpa), whose crown-ornaments are the forms of the highest lordship (pāramaiṣṭhya-rūpāṇi), and whose weapons are the five elemental realities (pañca-bhūta-tattva-svarūpāṇi). This entire coordinated assembly is the aggregate (samaṣṭi) cosmic-person form, standing eternal and unmoved (kūṭastha), the cause of all causes (sarva-kāraṇa-kāraṇa), intrinsically beyond the qualities (nirguṇa-bhūta). Kṛṣṇa displays his own incomparable splendor (nirupa-matejas) here as the ground of the manifest world — a grace-disclosure (prasāda) even in this fierce aspect.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'āmbaraṃ chandas-māya-rūpaṃ kirīṭādy-ābharaṇāni ca pāramaiṣṭhyādi-rūpāṇi; āyudhāni pañca-bhūta-tattva-svarūpāṇi; samaṣṭi-bhūta-puruṣa-svarūpa-bhūtaṃ kūṭasthaṃ sarva-kāraṇa-kāraṇaṃ nirguṇa-bhūtam.'
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha uniquely decodes each ornamental element cosmologically; the form is not symbolic wonder but a precise metaphysical inventory.

bhakti.rendering: Śrīdhara Svāmī stays close to the verbal surface: the Lord wears celestial garlands and garments (divyāni mālya-āmbarāṇi ca dhārayantīti), bears divine fragrant unguents (divyo gandho yasya tādṛśam anulepanaṃ yasya tat), is filled with every kind of wonder (aneka-āścarya-prāyam), is luminous by nature (deva — dyotana-ātmaka), is without boundary (ananta — aparicchinnam), and has faces in all directions (viśvataḥ sarvato mukhāni yasmin tat). The rendering is devotionally immediate: each epithet is a point of loving attention in the darśana of the cosmic form, inviting the devotee to rest their gaze on each element in sequence.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'devam dyotanātmakam, anantam aparichinnam, viśvataḥ sarvato mukhāni yasmin tat' — literal glosses oriented toward devotional perception.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara does not allegorize or polemicize; the form is taken as the direct object of bhakti-vision (darśana).

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī carefully unpacks the compound epithets: the Lord wears garlands made of flowers and gems (puṣpa-maya and ratna-maya), divine garments, and fragrant unguents that are themselves divine (divyo gandho asya iti divya-gandhas tad-anulepanaṃ yasya tat). He is filled with many wonders (aneka-adbhuta-pracura), luminous (deva — dyotana-ātmaka), truly limitless (ananta — aparicchinnam), with faces everywhere (viśvataḥ sarvato mukhāni yasmin). The verse connects back syntactically: 'Sañjaya showed (darśayāmāsa)' or 'Arjuna saw (adadarśa).' Here jñāna and bhakti converge: the infinite form is both the non-dual Absolute made perceptible and the beloved Lord whose very clothing is adorned with love.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'divyāni mālayāni puṣpamayāni ratnamayāni ca; anantam aparicchinnam; viśvataḥ sarvato mukhāni yasmin tad-rūpaṃ darśayāmāseti pūrveṇa saṃbandhaḥ.'
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana alone notes both syntactic options (Sañjaya showed / Arjuna saw) and explicitly layers the gem-garland detail absent in other commentators.

so_what_questions: If the Lord's faces point in every direction (viśvato-mukha), what does it mean for a practitioner to seek a single 'correct' direction in which to face while worshipping?

Vallabha decodes the garments as Vedic metres and the weapons as the five elements — what does it do to one's practice when ritual objects are understood as literal constituents of the cosmos rather than symbols pointing beyond themselves?

Śaṅkara grounds the boundlessness (ananta) in self-identity with all beings (sarva-bhūta-ātma-bhūtatva) while Rāmānuja grounds it in being the support of all three times — what different ethical obligations flow from each reading?
This verse is a description within a vision (darśana) that Arjuna found overwhelming; at what point does wonder (āścarya) become a barrier to rather than a gateway into understanding?
Madhva compresses his commentary to a single gloss; does extreme economy of doctrinal comment itself carry a theological message about what exceeds language in this form?

The form is adorned (divya-mālya-āmbara-dhara) and fragrant (divya-gandha-anulepana) — sensory qualities — yet it is boundless and universal-faced; how do schools that emphasize divine transcendence handle the irreducibly embodied, sensory character of this description?

If this cosmic form represents an aggregate (samaṣṭi) rather than an individual, does Arjuna's personal terror in response to it (later verses) tell us something about how the individual self (vyaṣṭi) confronts its own ground?

everyday_applications.advaita: When your sense of being a bounded 'me' feels most solid — in competition, fear, or pride — recall viśvato-mukha: awareness itself has no single facing direction, no edge, no back. The moment you notice the noticing, you have touched the non-dual ground this verse points at. The practice is not to feel cosmic but to recognize that every face you see today is, in the only sense that survives analysis, your own.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Every sincere act of service (kainkarya) — to a colleague, a stranger, a project done with care — is participation in the Lord's body, which Rāmānuja says encompasses every direction. Treat the people and situations you encounter today as belonging to Bhagavān's 'body' that you are serving, not as obstacles or instruments for your agenda. This reframe converts ordinary work into the devotional preparation for bhakti-yoga that Rāmānuja prescribes.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Genuine wonder (āścarya) in front of something far greater than yourself — a vast landscape, a piece of music, a moral act of real courage — is Hari's own wonder-nature (sarva-āścarya-ātmaka) briefly visible to you. Do not domesticate it with explanation. Madhva's terse reverence before this verse is itself a practice: sometimes the correct response to reality is silence and acknowledgment of dependence, not commentary.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's reading that the Lord's garment is the Vedic metres suggests that language — when it is precise, rhythmically alive, and truthful — is itself a form of divine clothing. In your own speech today, notice the difference between language used as utility and language offered with care. The latter is closer to chandas-māya-rūpa; it participates in the cosmic fabric Vallabha is describing.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's sequential glossing (garlands, fragrance, wonder, luminosity, boundlessness, universal faces) models a specific contemplative practice: slow, attentive enumeration rather than grasping at the whole at once. Apply this to something you find beautiful or overwhelming today — a relationship, a problem, a piece of art. Move through it element by element. The devotional gaze is patient; it does not rush to conclusion.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's care in distinguishing flower-garlands from gem-garlands — a small philological precision — is itself a teaching: love expresses itself through detail. In whatever you are devoted to (a person, a craft, a practice), the quality of your attention to fine particulars is the measure of your love, not the grandeur of your stated commitment. The infinite and the intimate are the same Lord.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present and usable; Madhva minimal but honest; Śrīdhara clean of HTML artifacts; renderings doctrinally divergent on the status of divine ornaments, the meaning of ananta, and the ontology of viśvato-mukha; no Sanskrit roots given English suffixes; concise.
